one_line_takeaway: UniMark shows that replacing a static, once-and-for-all red/green codebook partition with a per-position, key-derived partition can simultaneously add multi-bit messaging, hold or improve image quality/detection accuracy, and cut worst-case partition-exposure forgery success from 68% to under 2%, across both next-token (LlamaGen, Open-MAGVIT2) and next-scale (VAR) autoregressive image generators.
key_finding: UniMark simultaneously improves or matches baselines on quality and detection while adding a multi-bit capability the baselines lack, and its key-dependent partition is markedly more forgery-resistant than IndexMark's static partition. Concretely: on LlamaGen, FID goes from 3.75 (no watermark) to 3.82 for UniMark (Delta FID 0.07) vs. 3.91 for IndexMark (Delta 0.16) and 4.12 for AR-Watermark (Delta 0.37), with UniMark's PSNR/SSIM (39.1/0.985) exceeding both baselines. Zero-bit TPR@FPR=1% for UniMark is 99.8%/99.9%/99.7% on LlamaGen/VAR/Open-MAGVIT2, slightly above IndexMark (99.7/99.9/99.5) and AR-Watermark (99.2/99.6/98.8). Multi-bit extraction accuracy (unique to UniMark) is >99% at B=16 and B=32 bits across all three models, and stays above 95% at B=64 bits for LlamaGen and VAR (94.8% for Open-MAGVIT2). Under the six-attack robustness suite on LlamaGen, UniMark's TPR/bit-accuracy (e.g., JPEG 97.5%/96.8%, cropping 91.3%/89.6%) exceeds IndexMark (JPEG TPR 96.2%, cropping TPR 88.5%, no bit-accuracy support) and AR-Watermark (JPEG TPR 93.1%, cropping TPR 82.6%) at every attack type. The component ablation (Table 6) shows the BCH error-correcting code is the single most impactful component for multi-bit reliability: removing it drops bit accuracy from 99.2% to 93.6% (a 5.6 percentage-point loss), while removing ASG (reverting to a static partition) mainly hurts image quality (Delta FID rises from 0.07 to 0.16). The security experiment shows forgery success rate against partial partition exposure reaches 68% for IndexMark's static scheme at 50% exposure, vs. below 2% for UniMark's adaptive per-position partition. Measured overhead is small: 8-15 ms per image, 1.2-1.9% relative to base generation time.
core_contribution: UniMark is a training-free, unified watermarking framework for autoregressive (AR) image generators (LlamaGen, VAR, Open-MAGVIT2). It combines three components introduced by the authors: (1) Adaptive Semantic Grouping (ASG) - a key-dependent, position-aware codebook partitioning scheme where a per-position pseudorandom permutation seeded by H(key||position) defines green/red token sets, and out-of-green tokens are replaced by their most semantically similar (cosine-similarity) in-green codebook entry, replacing the prior art's single static red/green partition; (2) Block-wise Multi-bit Encoding (BME) - the token sequence is split into n blocks, a BCH error-correcting code encodes a B-bit message into n bits, and each block is biased toward the green or red set depending on its corresponding encoded bit, with extraction done by thresholding the per-block green-token ratio and BCH-decoding; (3) a Unified Token-Replacement Interface (UTRI) - an abstraction (GetTokenSequence / ReplaceTokens / DecodeImage) that lets the same embedding logic run on both next-token models (e.g., LlamaGen, raster-order 1D sequence) and next-scale models (e.g., VAR, concatenated per-scale token maps with block boundaries aligned to scales). The paper also proves a closed-form false-positive-rate bound for zero-bit detection (Theorem 1, via CLT on the green-token indicator) and a capacity bound Bmax = floor(N/Nmin)*R_BCH relating embeddable bits to token sequence length (Theorem 2).
threat_model: The paper's security argument targets an adversary who wants to forge or defeat the watermark by learning the red/green partition. It explicitly contrasts UniMark's per-position, key-derived partition against IndexMark's single global static partition: if a static partition is exposed (fully or partially), an adversary can replicate or strip the watermark, whereas under UniMark each position i uses an independently derived partition from H(key||i), so partial exposure of some positions gives no information about the partition at other positions. The paper quantifies this with a partition-exposure-vs-forgery-success experiment (Figure 4b): at 50% of positions exposed, IndexMark's forgery success rate reaches 68%, while UniMark's stays below 2%. Separately, the paper evaluates robustness (not adversarial evasion per se, but signal-processing perturbations) against six image transformations: JPEG compression (quality 50), Gaussian noise (sigma=0.05), Gaussian blur (kernel 5, sigma=1.0), random cropping (75% area retained), color jitter (brightness/contrast +/-0.2), and random erasing (10% area). The theoretical false-positive-rate analysis (Theorem 1) formalizes detection error under the null hypothesis of an unwatermarked image, which is a standard content-provenance/anti-spoofing style guarantee. Note: the paper's own Conclusion lists 'robustness against adversarial regeneration attacks' as future work, not something evaluated in this paper - no regeneration-attack experiments appear in the results sections.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Conclusion) is that embedding capacity is fundamentally bounded by token sequence length and codebook size (formalized in Theorem 2), so higher-capacity messages require longer sequences or larger codebooks; the authors list adaptive per-content capacity allocation, extension to video AR generators, and evaluation against adversarial regeneration attacks as future work - meaning regeneration-attack robustness (mentioned as a goal in the record's abstract text) is NOT actually tested in this paper. Additional limitations observable from the text itself: (1) the named baseline Tree-Ring does not appear with quantitative results in any extracted table, so the claimed three-way comparison is not fully substantiated in the material reviewed; (2) all evaluation is on ImageNet-conditioned generation at 256x256 resolution with only three specific AR backbones, so generalization to text-to-image AR models or higher resolutions is untested; (3) the false-positive-rate proof (Theorem 1) assumes tokens are generated independently of the key-derived partition, which the authors state follows because the partition depends only on H(key||i) - this is a reasonable but unverified independence assumption, not empirically stress-tested against a model that might correlate its outputs with public partition-derivation algorithms; (4) capacity/robustness results are reported only for gamma=0.5 and B=32 as the primary robustness configuration (Table 4/7/8), with the broader gamma sweep run only for FID/TPR/bit-accuracy without full robustness re-evaluation at each gamma.

datasets_used: ImageNet validation classes (5,000 generated images per model, per the paper's experimental setup)
quantitative_results: Table 1 - FID/PSNR/SSIM, LlamaGen: no-watermark FID 3.75; IndexMark FID 3.91 (DeltaFID 0.16), PSNR 38.2, SSIM 0.981; AR-Watermark FID 4.12 (Delta 0.37), PSNR 36.8, SSIM 0.972; UniMark FID 3.82 (Delta 0.07), PSNR 39.1, SSIM 0.985

Table 1 - VAR: no-watermark FID 2.95; IndexMark FID 3.18 (Delta 0.23), PSNR 37.5, SSIM 0.978; AR-Watermark FID 3.35 (Delta 0.40), PSNR 35.9, SSIM 0.968; UniMark FID 3.04 (Delta 0.09), PSNR 38.6, SSIM 0.983

Table 1 - Open-MAGVIT2: no-watermark FID 3.42; IndexMark FID 3.61 (Delta 0.19), PSNR 38.8, SSIM 0.983; AR-Watermark FID 3.89 (Delta 0.47), PSNR 36.2, SSIM 0.970; UniMark FID 3.50 (Delta 0.08), PSNR 39.5, SSIM 0.986
Table 2 - Zero-bit TPR@FPR=1%: IndexMark 99.7%/99.9%/99.5% (LlamaGen/VAR/Open-MAGVIT2); AR-Watermark 99.2%/99.6%/98.8%; UniMark 99.8%/99.9%/99.7%
Table 3 - UniMark multi-bit extraction accuracy by message length (LlamaGen/VAR/Open-MAGVIT2): B=16: 99.8/99.9/99.7; B=32: 99.2/99.6/99.1; B=48: 97.5/98.8/97.1; B=64: 95.3/97.2/94.8

Table 4 - LlamaGen robustness (TPR@FPR=1% / 32-bit Bit Accuracy) under JPEG/Noise/Blur/Crop/Color: IndexMark TPR 96.2/94.8/97.1/88.5/97.8 (no bit-accuracy support); AR-Watermark TPR 93.1/91.2/95.3/82.6/95.1; UniMark TPR 97.5/96.1/98.2/91.3/98.5, Bit Acc 96.8/95.2/97.5/89.6/97.9

Table 5 - green ratio gamma sweep on LlamaGen: gamma=0.3 FID 3.78 (Delta 0.03), TPR 97.2, BitAcc 95.8; gamma=0.5 FID 3.82 (Delta 0.07), TPR 99.8, BitAcc 99.2; gamma=0.7 FID 3.95 (Delta 0.20), TPR 99.9, BitAcc 99.7

Table 6 - component ablation on LlamaGen (B=32): full UniMark FID 3.82/TPR 99.8/BitAcc 99.2; w/o ASG (static partition) FID 3.91/TPR 99.5/BitAcc 98.8; w/o BME (zero-bit only) FID 3.80/TPR 99.8; w/o Error Correction FID 3.82/TPR 99.8/BitAcc 93.6 (a 5.6-point drop from full)
Security analysis (Figure 4b): forgery success rate at 50% partition exposure - IndexMark (static partition) 68%, UniMark (adaptive partition) below 2%
Efficiency (Figure 5): watermark embedding overhead 8-15 ms per image, 1.2-1.9% relative to base generation time (approx. 1.4% LlamaGen, 1.9% VAR, 1.2% Open-MAGVIT2)
Table 7 - UniMark robustness on VAR (32-bit): TPR 98.2/97.5/98.8/93.6/99.1 (JPEG/Noise/Blur/Crop/Color); Bit Acc 97.5/96.8/98.1/91.2/98.3
Table 8 - UniMark robustness on Open-MAGVIT2 (32-bit): TPR 96.8/95.3/97.5/89.8/98.0; Bit Acc 96.1/94.5/96.9/88.3/97.2
Appendix A.2 - BCH code table: B=16 uses BCH(31,16,7), rate 0.516; B=32 uses BCH(63,36,5), rate 0.571; B=48 uses BCH(63,51,5), rate 0.810; B=64 uses BCH(127,64,21), rate 0.504
baselines_compared: IndexMark (Tong et al., 2025) - training-free, zero-bit, static red/green codebook partition; has numeric results in Tables 1, 2, 4
AR-Watermark (Jovanovic et al., 2025) - LLM-style red-green watermark extended to AR image tokens; has numeric results in Tables 1, 2, 4

Tree-Ring (Wen et al., 2023) - diffusion-model watermark applied via regeneration; named as a baseline in the experimental setup section but no corresponding results row was found in the extracted result tables
